title: Fortinet FortiClient EMS Zero-Day CVE-2026-35616 (CVSS 9.1): Unauthenticated API Bypass Enables RCE on Endpoint Management Platform — Active Exploitation Confirmed via watchTowr Honeypots, Emergency Hotfixes Released April 4

Help Net Security — FortiClient EMS zero-day exploited, emergency hotfixes available

timeline.event: watchTowr honeypots detect first exploitation attempts targeting FortiClient EMS API endpoints; Defused Cyber researchers (Simo Kohonen, Nguyen Duc Anh) confirm zero-day exploitation in the wild
CVE-2026-35616 reserved and published on NVD; CVSS 9.8 assigned by NVD CNA
Fortinet publishes advisory FG-IR-26-099 and releases emergency hotfixes: 7.4.5.2111 for FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6.2170 for 7.4.6; security media coverage begins
At least 2 public proof-of-concept exploits published on GitHub; over 2,000 exposed FortiClient EMS instances tracked by Shadowserver; ongoing active exploitation confirmed

simple_summary.what_happened: Fortinet issued emergency security patches for FortiClient EMS — the server-side component that manages endpoint security policies for FortiClient agents deployed across an organization's devices. A critical vulnerability was discovered in versions 7.4.5 and 7.4.6 that allows any attacker on the internet to bypass API authentication entirely and execute arbitrary commands on the server without providing any credentials. What makes this particularly dangerous is the timing: attackers began exploiting it before Fortinet even knew about the vulnerability (a zero-day), targeting the Easter holiday weekend when security teams are typically reduced. Researchers detected exploitation attempts starting March 31, and Fortinet only released patches on April 4 — meaning organizations were exposed for at least 4 days with no vendor-provided fix.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Organizations using Fortinet FortiClient EMS versions 7.4.5 or 7.4.6 with the management web interface accessible from the network (and especially from the internet). Over 2,000 FortiClient EMS instances were publicly internet-accessible according to Shadowserver scanning, with 1,400+ in the United States alone. Any enterprise that relies on FortiClient for endpoint security management is at risk — compromising the EMS server gives attackers control over security policies across all enrolled endpoints.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker scans the internet for FortiClient EMS instances on standard ports (TCP 443/80) — Shadowserver data shows over 2,000 are publicly exposed. Step 2: Attacker crafts a specially formatted HTTP request targeting an API endpoint that bypasses the EMS authentication and authorization middleware, requiring no username or password. Step 3: The unauthenticated API request is accepted by the server as though it came from an authenticated administrator. Step 4: Attacker uses the authenticated API context to execute arbitrary code or OS commands on the FortiClient EMS server. Step 5: With control of the EMS server, attacker can push malicious configuration changes or malware to all FortiClient agents enrolled under this management server — turning a single server compromise into a fleet-wide endpoint breach.
what_you_should_do: Apply emergency hotfix immediately: upgrade FortiClient EMS 7.4.5 to build 7.4.5.2111 and FortiClient EMS 7.4.6 to build 7.4.6.2170 — download from Fortinet Support portal
If running FortiClient EMS 7.2.x: no action needed for this specific CVE (not affected)
Immediately restrict network access to the EMS management interface — it should never be internet-accessible; place behind VPN or on an isolated management network
Audit EMS access logs retroactively from March 31 onward for unauthenticated API requests and any unexpected admin activity or configuration changes
After patching, plan upgrade to FortiClient EMS 7.4.7 when released, which includes the full integrated fix rather than the emergency hotfix
Review all managed FortiClient endpoints for unauthorized policy changes or deployed payloads that may have been pushed by a compromised EMS server
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: FortiClient EMS sits at the top of an organization's endpoint security hierarchy — it manages and controls security policies for every enrolled FortiClient device. Compromising it doesn't just give attackers access to one server; it provides a command-and-control hub over all managed endpoints. This was actively exploited as a zero-day before any patch was available, giving attackers a head start. The timing around Easter weekend (when security teams are reduced) was assessed as deliberate. Public proof-of-concept exploits were published within 24 hours of the patch release, which will dramatically accelerate exploitation attempts by less sophisticated threat actors. The predecessor vulnerability (CVE-2026-21643) affecting FortiClient EMS 7.4.4 was already being briefed — this is the same product line being hit again with a bypass that survived the previous patch.

tactics.description: FortiClient EMS web and API interface is directly exploited over the network. Over 2,000 instances are internet-exposed, providing a large attack surface. No credentials required — the API bypass works pre-authentication.
After bypassing authentication, attackers can execute arbitrary OS commands on the FortiClient EMS server through the compromised API context.

The FortiClient EMS server is a software deployment and management platform. Once compromised, it can be abused to push malicious configurations, executables, or policy changes to all FortiClient-enrolled endpoints across the organization.
An attacker controlling the EMS server can modify or disable FortiClient endpoint security policies, effectively neutralizing the organization's endpoint protection across all managed devices.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection should focus on three layers: identifying exposed and unpatched EMS instances, monitoring API access logs for unauthenticated requests, and detecting post-exploitation behaviors on the EMS server. Given active exploitation since March 31, retroactive log review is essential — organizations may already be compromised.

log_sources.source: FortiClient EMS Web Server / API Access Logs
log_sources.description: Review Apache/IIS access logs on the EMS server for API requests that lack valid session cookies or tokens, particularly targeting management API endpoints. Look for anomalous POST/PUT requests from unfamiliar IP addresses.
Review EMS audit logs for unexpected administrator actions, configuration changes, or policy deployments — especially from sessions that cannot be correlated with known administrator IPs or times.
Monitor the FortiClient EMS server host for unexpected process execution, new service installations, or outbound connections to unusual destinations that could indicate post-exploitation activity.

Review inbound connections to the EMS management interface from external/internet IP ranges — EMS should only be accessible from internal management networks. Any internet access is a misconfiguration that may already be under exploitation.
Use runZero asset query to detect exposed FortiClient EMS instances: `_asset.protocol:http AND protocol:http AND favicon.ico.image.mmh3:=-800551065`. Use this to confirm exposure scope.

what_to_look_for.description: Any EMS installation on these unpatched builds is vulnerable. Identify via Fortinet Support portal version check or asset inventory.
what_to_look_for.log_field: product='FortiClientEMS' AND version IN ('7.4.5','7.4.6') AND build < hotfix_threshold

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unauthenticated API requests to EMS endpoints
what_to_look_for.description: HTTP requests to EMS API paths that lack authentication headers/cookies, especially POST/PUT requests that would normally require admin privileges.
what_to_look_for.log_field: http_method IN ('POST','PUT') AND request_path CONTAINS '/api/' AND auth_cookie IS NULL AND http_status=200

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unexpected process spawning on EMS server
what_to_look_for.description: Processes not normally launched by FortiClient EMS service — particularly cmd.exe, powershell.exe, bash, or network tools — indicate post-exploitation activity.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Unusual FortiClient policy pushes or configuration changes
what_to_look_for.description: Bulk policy changes or new executable deployment to enrolled FortiClient agents occurring outside change management windows could indicate an attacker leveraging EMS as a distribution mechanism.
what_to_look_for.log_field: ems_audit: action='PolicyDeploy' AND initiated_by_ip NOT IN known_admin_ips

immediate_actions.action: Apply emergency hotfix for FortiClient EMS
immediate_actions.description: Download and install the emergency hotfix build from Fortinet Support portal immediately. For FortiClient EMS 7.4.5: upgrade to build 7.4.5.2111. For FortiClient EMS 7.4.6: upgrade to build 7.4.6.2170. If running 7.2.x, you are not affected by this CVE. FortiClient EMS 7.4.7 (with integrated fix) will be the permanent resolution.

immediate_actions.action: Isolate EMS management interface from the internet
immediate_actions.description: The FortiClient EMS web interface should never be directly internet-accessible. Place it behind a VPN or on an isolated out-of-band management network reachable only from authorized administrator workstations. Block inbound access on ports 443/80 to the EMS server from any untrusted network segment.
immediate_actions.command:
# Verify no internet access: check firewall rules for inbound access to EMS IP on 443/80
# Temporarily block if exposed:
iptables -I INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -s 0.0.0.0/0 ! -s <management-subnet> -j DROP

immediate_actions.action: Conduct retroactive forensic review from March 31 onward
immediate_actions.description: Exploitation began March 31, 2026 — before any patch existed. Review all EMS access logs, audit trails, and endpoint policy change logs from March 31 to present. Look for unauthenticated API access, unauthorized admin activity, or unexpected policy deployments. Treat any suspicious findings as potential active compromise.
immediate_actions.command:
# On Windows FortiClient EMS server — check IIS logs for anomalous requests
Get-Content C:\inetpub\logs\LogFiles\W3SVC1\*.log | Where-Object { $_ -match '/api/' -and $_ -notmatch 'authenticated' }

immediate_actions.action: Audit enrolled endpoints for unauthorized changes
immediate_actions.description: If EMS was compromised, attackers may have pushed malicious configurations or executables to enrolled FortiClient agents. Audit all managed endpoints for unexpected security policy changes, new scheduled tasks, new executables, or unusual network connections since March 31.

immediate_actions.action: Monitor threat intelligence for CISA KEV addition
immediate_actions.description: As of April 4, CVE-2026-35616 is not yet in the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, but active exploitation makes CISA KEV listing likely. Federal agencies and organizations with federal contracts should monitor and be prepared for an accelerated patching mandate.
